name: Standard Smart Pointers, span, observer_ptr
summary: C++ vocabulary types encoding ownership and aliasing intent — the baseline that the Profiles approach to memory safety enforces against. `unique_ptr`/`shared_ptr`/`weak_ptr` are RAII heap ownership; `std::span` (C++20) is the canonical replacement for `T*, size_t` pairs; `observer_ptr<T>` (N4282) was never standardized.

sections.title: Overview
sections.content:
C++'s ownership and aliasing story is expressed through library-level vocabulary types rather than language-level affinity. `unique_ptr` / `shared_ptr` / `weak_ptr` are RAII-based heap ownership; recognized by Clang's lifetime analysis as Owner/Pointer types and form the substrate that Profiles enforce against. `std::span` (C++20) is the canonical replacement for `T*, size_t` pairs; in hardened libc++ its iterators carry bounds. `observer_ptr<T>` (proposed N4282, "world's dumbest smart pointer" expressing non-owning intent) was never standardized; stuck in `std::experimental` after years; the Profiles era reduces motivation.

The lesson: C++'s ownership story is library-level vocabulary types, not language-level affinity — and the Profiles approach doubles down on that choice rather than introducing language-level move-only or borrow-checked references.

sections.title: Owner / Pointer categories for Lifetime Profile
sections.content:
These vocabulary types matter beyond their RAII semantics because the Lifetime Profile (P1179) classifies them as Owner versus Pointer types. `unique_ptr` and `shared_ptr` are Owners; `weak_ptr` and `std::span` are Pointers. The classification is what allows local-only static analysis to detect dangling without whole-program annotation: an Owner's destruction invalidates any Pointer derived from it.

The `gsl::Owner` and `gsl::Pointer` attributes (and Clang's `lifetimebound`) refine the inference where the heuristic alone is insufficient. This is the substrate the Profiles approach is built on — language safety as a refinement of vocabulary-typed library code.

sections.title: observer_ptr's fate
sections.content: `observer_ptr<T>` was proposed (N4282) as a non-owning pointer type whose role was to express "this is a pointer that does *not* own its target" in API signatures, where bare `T*` is ambiguous. It was never standardized; it has been parked in `std::experimental` for years. The Profiles era reduces its motivation further: if a Lifetime Profile already classifies bare pointers under Owner/Pointer categories from context, the explicit `observer_ptr` typename is redundant for the analysis. The result is that C++ never adopted a vocabulary type explicitly for non-owning intent — that role is absorbed by `T*` plus the Lifetime Profile classification.
